eli3: The context window is the maximum number of tokens a transformer can process in a single forward pass — both input tokens and any generated output tokens count toward this limit. GPT-2: 1024 tokens. GPT-3: 2048. GPT-4: 8K/32K/128K. Claude 3: 200K. Gemini 1.5 Pro: 1M. Beyond the context window, the model has no memory — earlier tokens are 'forgotten' unless retrieved externally.

The fundamental bottleneck is quadratic attention: self-attention computes an attention score between every pair of tokens in the sequence, so memory and compute both grow as the square of the sequence length. For 128K tokens, the naive attention matrix would require tens of gigabytes per head — impractical without approximation. Long-context transformers solve this with efficient attention algorithms (FlashAttention), better position encodings (RoPE, ALiBi), and sparse attention patterns.

Long context windows enable new use cases: entire codebases as context, full legal documents, book-length summarization, multi-document reasoning. But long context does not equal reliable recall — the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon: models recall information near the beginning and end of long contexts well, but information in the middle is often missed. Effective context use requires both architectural and prompting strategies.

whenWhy.title: Document and codebase processing
whenWhy.body: With 128K+ context windows (GPT-4 Turbo, Claude 3), entire large files or small codebases fit in a single prompt. Use cases: (1) summarizing a 300-page report; (2) Q&A over a full legal contract; (3) code review of an entire module; (4) few-shot learning with many examples. These tasks were previously impossible with 2K-4K context models and required retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) as a workaround. Long context can replace RAG for moderate-sized corpora.

whenWhy.title: Multi-turn conversation memory
whenWhy.body: Conversational AI systems concatenate conversation history as the context. With 4K tokens: only ~20 turns of history. With 128K: hundreds of turns, or full conversation logs. This enables coherent long-form interactions without external memory management. However, the quadratic attention cost means long conversations are expensive at inference — each token generation costs attention over the full history. KV-cache mitigates this per step, but total context KV-cache grows with conversation length.

whenWhy.title: In-context learning with many examples
whenWhy.body: Larger context windows enable many-shot ICL (Agarwal et al. 2024): providing thousands of examples in the prompt. Many-shot ICL dramatically improves accuracy on hard tasks — GPT-4 with 2048 examples on a hate speech task: 90% accuracy vs 60% for zero-shot. Long context allows overriding model priors for specialized domains. This is a practical alternative to fine-tuning when labeled data is available but fine-tuning is infeasible.

deepDiveIntro: Standard self-attention computes query, key, and value matrices where each row corresponds to one token. Computing the full attention matrix requires multiplying queries by keys, giving a matrix proportional to sequence length squared in size — both in memory and compute. For 128K tokens, this matrix alone would require tens of gigabytes just to store, making it infeasible without approximation. FlashAttention (Dao et al. 2022) solves this by tiling the computation into blocks that fit in the GPU's fast on-chip memory, achieving the same numerical result as standard attention but using only linear memory.

Position encoding is critical for long context because models must understand where each token sits in the sequence. Original absolute learned positions (used in BERT/GPT) cannot generalise beyond the training length. Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) encode position by rotating the query and key vectors, ensuring the attention score between any two tokens depends only on their relative distance — which generalises naturally to longer sequences. YaRN and similar methods extend RoPE beyond its training range by scaling the rotation frequencies.

deepDive.heading: FlashAttention for Efficient Long-Context Computation
deepDive.body: Standard attention is bottlenecked by reading and writing the full attention matrix from GPU memory (HBM), which dominates runtime even though the floating point operations per second are not fully utilised. FlashAttention (Dao et al. 2022) avoids materialising this matrix by processing attention in tiles that fit in the GPU's fast on-chip SRAM (similar to CPU L1/L2 cache). Using an online softmax algorithm, it produces the identical result as standard attention while reducing memory from quadratic to linear. FlashAttention is 2–4× faster than standard PyTorch attention and is now standard in all modern LLM training. [1]
deepDive.formula: Memory: O(n²) → O(n) with FlashAttention; runtime: same O(n²d) FLOPs but IO-optimal

deepDive.heading: Position Encoding and Context Extension
deepDive.body: RoPE (Su et al. 2021) encodes position by rotating query and key vectors at each attention head by an angle proportional to the position and the dimension index. The dot product between a query at position m and a key at position n then depends only on the relative distance m-n, not their absolute positions. This makes RoPE naturally suited for length generalisation. However, RoPE degrades when extended far beyond its training length. YaRN (Peng et al. 2023) extends RoPE by applying a scaling factor to the rotation frequencies along with an attention temperature adjustment — enabling models like Llama to extend from 4K to 128K or beyond without full retraining. [2]

deepDive.heading: Lost in the Middle: Recall Degradation
deepDive.body: Liu et al. (2023) evaluated LLMs on multi-document QA tasks requiring retrieval from a specific document within a long context. They found that performance was highest when the relevant document was at the beginning or end of the context (primacy and recency effects), and dropped significantly when it was in the middle. For example, GPT-3.5 on a 20-document context with the relevant document in the middle scored around 35% accuracy, compared to 70–80% when the document was at the start or end. Practical implication for RAG: put the most relevant retrieved chunks at the beginning or end of the context, not in the middle. [3]

deepDive.heading: KV-Cache and Inference Cost at Long Context
deepDive.body: At inference time, the key and value vectors for all past tokens are cached in GPU memory to avoid recomputing them at each step. This cache grows linearly with context length and number of layers, heads, and head dimension. For large models at very long contexts, the KV-cache can exceed single GPU memory. Solutions include: quantising the KV-cache to 4- or 8-bit integers, multi-query attention (sharing key and value matrices across all heads), and grouped-query attention (sharing across groups of heads) — the latter is used in Llama 2 for long-context inference. [2]

takeaways: Context window = max tokens in one forward pass; attention is O(n²) memory — the fundamental bottleneck. [1]
FlashAttention: tile-based computation, O(n) memory, 2–4× faster — standard in all modern LLMs. [1]
RoPE + YaRN: extend context beyond training length without full retraining. [2]
Lost in the middle: models recall beginning and end of context best — place key info at boundaries. [3]
KV-cache grows with context; MQA/GQA reduces memory for long-context inference. [2]

edgeCases.q: Does a longer context window always improve performance?
edgeCases.a: Not necessarily. (1) Lost in the middle: adding more context can hurt if the relevant information gets buried in the middle. (2) Distraction: irrelevant context in the prompt can confuse the model (Shi et al. 2023 — irrelevant information in math problems significantly hurts GPT-4's accuracy). (3) Cost: longer context means more expensive API calls and slower inference. (4) Out-of-distribution positions: models trained with RoPE interpolation may have degraded quality at the extreme end of their extended context (quality is best at or below the original training length). The optimal strategy is often still RAG with selective retrieval — put the most relevant ~2K tokens in context, not a full 128K dump. [3]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between sparse attention (Longformer/BigBird) and full attention with FlashAttention?
edgeCases.a: Sparse attention (Longformer, BigBird): reduce the quadratic number of attention operations by computing attention only over a subset of token pairs. Longformer uses a local sliding window plus a few global tokens, reducing compute to roughly linear in sequence length. BigBird adds random attention on top. Reduces both FLOPs and memory. Trade-off: misses long-range dependencies not captured by the sparse pattern. Full attention with FlashAttention: computes exact attention over all pairs — still quadratic in FLOPs but memory-efficient to linear. Preferred when sequence length is moderate (up to 128K) and accuracy matters. For extreme lengths (millions of tokens), sparse attention or state-space models (Mamba SSMs) become necessary. [1]

sources.label: Dao et al. (2022) — FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness, NeurIPS 2022
Su et al. (2021) — RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding
Liu et al. (2023) — Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts, TACL 2024
